title: Component API design

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Component API design; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Component API design
sources.claim: A component interface defines required and optional props, callbacks, controlled values, defaults, and semantic constraints.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A component interface defines required and optional props, callbacks, controlled values, defaults, and semantic constraints.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Button requires children and onPress while variant has a documented default and disabled behavior. Your goal is to review and simplify a component interface while documenting its contract. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Components and JSX; Children and composition; javascript modules architecture. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A component interface defines required and optional props, callbacks, controlled values, defaults, and semantic constraints. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Type checking cannot prove accessible labels, callback effects, or valid combinations without deliberate design. This draft therefore rejects: TypeScript prop types automatically guarantee the component is accessible and semantically valid.

includes: Keep component props minimal, cohesive, serializable where practical and semantically named.
Review and simplify a component interface while documenting its contract.
excludes: Type checking cannot prove accessible labels, callback effects, or valid combinations without deliberate design.

outcomes: Review and simplify a component interface while documenting its contract.

examples.title: Component API design: concrete case
examples.situation: A Button requires children and onPress while variant has a documented default and disabled behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A Button requires children and onPress while variant has a documented default and disabled behavior.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Types check shapes; document and test semantic contracts, accessibility, state, and callback behavior separately.
examples.result: The result follows because Types check shapes; document and test semantic contracts, accessibility, state, and callback behavior separately.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: TypeScript prop types automatically guarantee the component is accessible and semantically valid.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: TypeScript prop types automatically guarantee the component is accessible and semantically valid.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Component API design, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Component API design before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to review and simplify a component interface while documenting its contract. Keep this boundary in view: Type checking cannot prove accessible labels, callback effects, or valid combinations without deliberate design.

Why is this claim incorrect: TypeScript prop types automatically guarantee the component is accessible and semantically valid.
Apply checkpoint 'semantic contracts' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Render purity and Strict Mode”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
